dating.display: 1955–56 (Marriage, Succession, Minority & Guardianship, Adoptions & Maintenance Acts); Ambedkar's resignation over the stalled bill, 1951
dating.basis: Parliamentary record of the Republic of India.

summary: Independent India codified and reformed Hindu personal law by statute: monogamy required, divorce created, inter-caste marriage validated, daughters given inheritance rights (completed by the 2005 amendment's equal coparcenary). Ambedkar drafted the original consolidated bill and resigned as Law Minister when it stalled; Nehru pushed the split acts through after the 1952 elections.
whyItMatters: The largest single legal reform of Hindu practice in history — the tradition's rules of marriage and inheritance rewritten by democratic legislation, over orthodox objection, within a decade of independence. Ends the atlas's reform thread (Roy → Vidyāsāgar → Ambedkar → Parliament) with a documented structural change.
honestyNote: Reform was partial and contested: the acts apply only to Hindus (with Buddhists, Jains, Sikhs folded in — itself controversial), left gaps women's-rights scholarship documents, and the 'Hindu' definition in law is a modern legal artifact, not a theological one.

sources.label: The Hindu Marriage Act 1955; Hindu Succession Act 1956 (statute texts)
Williams, R.V., Postcolonial Politics and Personal Laws (OUP, 2006)
Ambedkar, B.R., resignation statement (10 Oct 1951), in Dr. Babasaheb Ambedkar: Writings and Speeches Vol. 14

image.alt: Statue of B. R. Ambedkar, principal drafter of the Hindu Code Bill

dharmicTie.statement: Democratic legislation operating on dharmaśāstra's historic domain (marriage, adoption, inheritance) — the latest layer in a jurisprudential lineage that runs from the smṛtis through commentary and Anglo-Hindu case law to Parliament.

dating.display: founded 1966, New York (A.C. Bhaktivedanta Swami Prabhupāda, arrived 1965)
dating.basis: Incorporation records and the movement's documented history.

summary: A 69-year-old Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇava monk sailed to New York with a trunk of books and founded the International Society for Krishna Consciousness — carrying Caitanya's 16th-century Bengali devotional lineage (see the Bhakti entry) into a worldwide movement of temples, the Bhagavad Gītā As It Is in 80+ languages, and 'Hare Krishna' as a global cultural fact.
whyItMatters: The clearest single demonstration that the medieval bhakti lineages remain live transmission chains, not museum pieces: an unbroken guru-paramparā operating on six continents. Also a real case study in diaspora religion's frictions and reforms (documented in the scholarship cited).
honestyNote: ISKCON's history includes serious, documented institutional failures after the founder's death (child-protection scandals the movement itself has published investigations of) — provenance discipline applies to the recent past too.

locations.name: 26 Second Avenue, New York — first storefront temple

sources.label: Bryant, E. & Ekstrand, M. (eds.), The Hare Krishna Movement: The Postcharismatic Fate of a Religious Transplant (Columbia, 2004)
Goswami, S.D., Śrīla Prabhupāda-līlāmṛta (multi-volume biography)
Rochford, E.B., Hare Krishna Transformed (NYU, 2007)

dharmicTie.statement: An unbroken Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇava guru-paramparā (from Caitanya) operating globally; its liturgy is the mahāmantra whose locus classicus is Upaniṣadic.

scriptureRefs.text: the Hare Kṛṣṇa mahāmantra's textual source

dating.display: UN General Assembly resolution 69/131 adopted 11 December 2014; first observance 21 June 2015
dating.basis: UN records: the resolution passed with a record 177 co-sponsoring member states.

summary: The UN designated the June solstice as International Day of Yoga after India's proposal — the fastest, most co-sponsored cultural resolution of its kind — with the first Delhi observance setting Guinness records for the largest yoga session. Yoga practice worldwide is now measured in the hundreds of millions.
whyItMatters: State-level, documentable evidence of the tradition's contemporary global footprint — the endpoint of the through-line that starts at Patañjali's three āsana sūtras. It also illustrates the modern entanglement of heritage, soft power, and practice that the ledger tracks when claims inflate.
honestyNote: A diplomatic observance is evidence of reach, not of antiquity or efficacy; health claims for yoga stand or fall on clinical literature, and the practice being celebrated is the modern synthesis documented in the Early Modern entry.

locations.name: United Nations, New York; first mass observance: Rajpath, New Delhi

sources.label: UN General Assembly resolution A/RES/69/131 (2014)
UN press coverage of the resolution and co-sponsorship record (2014)
Singleton, M. & Goldberg, E. (eds.), Gurus of Modern Yoga (OUP, 2013) — the modern context

dharmicTie.statement: Global observance of a practice whose charter texts sit inside the tradition's own canon.

scriptureRefs.text: yuktāhāra-vihārasya — yoga as the discipline of the measured life

title.en: The idol-trafficking cases and artifact repatriations

dating.display: Kapoor network exposed from 2011 (Operation Hidden Idol); major returns 2014–present (incl. 307 objects from Manhattan, 2022; Australia's NGA returns)
dating.basis: Court records (Subhash Kapoor convicted, Kumbakonam court 2022), Manhattan DA repatriation announcements, National Gallery of Australia provenance decisions.

summary: Investigations into the Manhattan dealer Subhash Kapoor's network exposed industrial-scale theft of temple sculpture — including living-worship Chola bronzes stolen from Tamil Nadu village temples — laundered through fabricated provenance into major museums. The fallout: hundreds of objects returned to India, museums forced into provenance research, and citizen projects (India Pride Project) crowd-matching stolen idols to auction catalogs.
whyItMatters: Provenance — this project's design principle — operating as law and activism in the present: the same evidence disciplines (photographs, records, matching) that vet an atlas entry also bring a god home. It also grounds the custodianship debates (see the Dancing Girl and the 'custodian' tweets in the ledger) in current, documented practice.
honestyNote: Repatriation is politically charged in all directions; this entry records convictions and completed returns, not the broader restitution arguments. Numbers change frequently — treat totals as of-date snapshots.

locations.name: Sripuranthan & Suthamalli temples, Ariyalur District, Tamil Nadu — key theft sites

sources.label: Manhattan District Attorney's Office, repatriation press releases (2022) — incl. the 307-object return
Vijay Kumar, S., The Idol Thief (Juggernaut, 2018)
National Gallery of Australia, Asian Art Provenance Project findings (2014–21)
India Pride Project — citizen provenance-matching that drove several returns (S. Vijay Kumar et al.)

image.alt: The idol-trafficking cases and artifact repatriations
image.credit: Richard Mortel from Riyadh, Saudi Arabia

dharmicTie.statement: The law itself recognized the tie: in the London Bumper case (1991), the pillaged Pathur Naṭarāja was returned because Indian law treats the consecrated deity as a juristic person — the idol sued, and won. These are not 'artworks' in the tradition's own terms; they are installed deities (prāṇapratiṣṭhā) taken from active worship.

scriptureRefs.text: consecration that makes an image a deity in worship — the basis of the juristic-person doctrine

title.en: Radiocarbon dating of the Bakhshālī manuscript

dating.display: Bodleian announcement September 2017; scholarly response the same year
dating.basis: The dating event itself is fully documented (the manuscript's date remains contested — see the zero entry).

summary: The Bodleian Library carbon-dated three folios of the Bakhshālī manuscript — the birch-bark mathematical text with the dot zero — and announced dates spanning 224–993 CE, headlined worldwide as 'zero is 500 years older than thought'. Within weeks, five specialists in Indian mathematics published a detailed rebuttal arguing the manuscript is codicologically one object and the split dates cannot each date the writing.
whyItMatters: A perfect live specimen of the atlas's central discipline: hard science, institutional press amplification, and philological pushback, all on the record. The lab result is data; the headline was an interpretation; the argument continues — and tathya's job is to show all three layers.
honestyNote: This entry documents the EVENT; the substantive dating question lives in the zero/place-value entry with both positions. Media 'oldest zero' league tables routinely ignore the rebuttal — a stock example for the detection pipeline's fabricated/inflated-authority patterns.

locations.name: Bodleian Library, Oxford, UK — MS. Sansk. d. 14

sources.label: Bodleian Libraries, "Carbon dating finds Bakhshali manuscript contains oldest recorded origins of the symbol 'zero'" (Sept 2017)
Plofker, Keller, Hayashi, Montelle & Sarma, History of Science in South Asia 5 (2017)
Hayashi, T., The Bakhshālī Manuscript: An Ancient Indian Mathematical Treatise (Groningen, 1995)

image.alt: Radcliffe Camera, Bodleian Libraries, Oxford — where the Bakhshālī manuscript is held

dharmicTie.statement: The manuscript under the microscope is a Sanskrit mathematical text of the siddhānta world — the dating fight is a modern chapter in the tradition's own knowledge history.

title.en: Digital Sanskrit — corpora and computational grammar

dating.display: 1990s–present (GRETIL from 2001; Sanskrit Heritage engine from ~2002; Digital Corpus of Sanskrit; SARIT)

summary: The Sanskrit corpus is going machine-readable: GRETIL's e-texts, SARIT's TEI-encoded editions, Hellwig's Digital Corpus of Sanskrit (morphologically tagged), and Gérard Huet's Sanskrit Heritage engine — a computational implementation of Pāṇinian morphology that segments and parses sandhi-joined text. Modern NLP treats Sanskrit as a serious test case (dependency treebanks, word-segmentation shared tasks).
whyItMatters: Pāṇini's formal grammar is now literally running as software — the cleanest contemporary vindication of the through-line in his atlas entry (and the honest version of every 'Sanskrit and computers' meme the ledger debunks). Open corpora also make tathya-style source-checking dramatically easier.
honestyNote: None of this makes Sanskrit 'the best language for AI' (see the NASA-Sanskrit ledger entry); it makes it a well-formalized language with excellent ancient tooling. Coverage is uneven and OCR/encoding of manuscripts remains a bottleneck.

throughLines.to: Computational linguistics in daily scholarly use
chain: Pāṇini's Aṣṭādhyāyī: generative rule system for Sanskrit
Huet's Sanskrit Heritage platform and successors implement Pāṇinian analysis computationally (from ~2002)
Tagged corpora (DCS) and shared tasks make Sanskrit NLP a live research field

sources.label: Huet, G., "A Functional Toolkit for Morphological and Phonological Processing" and the Sanskrit Heritage site
Hellwig, O., Digital Corpus of Sanskrit
GRETIL — Göttingen Register of Electronic Texts in Indian Languages
Huet, G., Sanskrit Heritage engine (INRIA)
Hellwig, O., Digital Corpus of Sanskrit
National Mission for Manuscripts (NAMAMI, Govt. of India) — the national manuscript census and digitization program

image.alt: Devanāgarī manuscript page (Praśna Upaniṣad) — the kind of text digital corpora encode

dharmicTie.statement: The vedāṅga of grammar, executing: Pāṇini's rule system now runs as software that parses the tradition's corpus daily — the liveliest possible sense of a living knowledge system.

scriptureRefs.ref: Aṣṭādhyāyī (as implemented in the Sanskrit Heritage engine)
scriptureRefs.text: the grammar itself, in daily computational use

dating.display: UNESCO ICH inscription 2017; the gathering's documented modern history runs through colonial records to the present (2019 Prayagraj; 2025 Mahākumbh)
dating.basis: The festival cycle is documented continuously in the modern period; its ANTIQUITY as 'the Kumbh' specifically is a genuine historical question (see honesty note).

summary: The rotating riverbank festival at Prayagraj, Haridwar, Nashik, and Ujjain — at its peaks the largest human gathering on Earth (official estimates for Prayagraj 2019 exceeded 120 million visits across the season; 2025 claims ran far higher). Inscribed by UNESCO as Intangible Cultural Heritage (2017). A city of tents, akhāṛās, and pilgrims materializes on a floodplain and disappears.
whyItMatters: Living Hindu practice at civilizational scale, in the present tense — the atlas's closing exhibit that this is a functioning tradition, not an archaeological one. Also a crowd-science and urban-logistics research site (Harvard's 2013 Kumbh study).
honestyNote: Two honesty flags: attendance numbers are official estimates with real methodological softness (count-inflation is routine); and 'the Kumbh is millennia old' compresses a complex history — bathing festivals at Prayāga are ancient (Xuanzang describes one, 7th c.), but the four-site Kumbh system as such is documented in its present form mainly from the early modern period (Maclean's study). Both the depth and the construction are real.

locations.name: Triveni Sangam, Prayagraj, Uttar Pradesh, India

sources.label: UNESCO ICH: Kumbh Mela (inscribed 2017)
Maclean, K., Pilgrimage and Power: The Kumbh Mela in Allahabad, 1765–1954 (OUP, 2008)
Mehrotra, R. & Vera, F., Kumbh Mela: Mapping the Ephemeral Megacity (Harvard, 2015)
IGNCA Kumbh documentation (Ministry of Culture)

dharmicTie.statement: The largest recurring act of Sanātana Dharma on Earth. The bathing sanctity of Prayāga is ancient (the confluence-bathing verse tradition; Xuanzang describes the gathering in the 7th century); the specific amṛta-kumbha framing draws on the purāṇic churning of the ocean, with its modern four-site system honestly dated in this entry.

scriptureRefs.text: the celebrated verse on bathing where the white and dark rivers meet — quoted in the Mahābhārata's praise of Prayāga
the churning narrative behind the amṛta-kumbha framing (its link to the melā is late — stated honestly)
